Conflict of Interest Code
for Assembly Staff
Dear Assemblyman McCarthy:
In early April of this year, we discussed the applica—
bility of the Political Reform Act, which requires adoption
of a Conflict of Interest Code by all state agencies,
to
the California Legislature.
At that
time,
the propriety
of the Act requiring submission of the
legislative Codes
to the Commission for review and approval was raised.
You
urged the Commission to seek an Attcrney Generel's opinlol,
on the constitutional issue raised.
We agreed to do
so.
To allow time for receipt and review of the opinion,
and
without prejudice to the merits of any issue raised by the
Legislature,
the Commission extended the deadline for sub-
mission DE 5 Conflict of Interest Code by the Legislature.
In the meanwhile, Senate Concurrent Resolution
106,
which
embodies
a Conflict of Interest Code
for officérs and
employees of the Legislature, was introduced and passed
by the Legislature.
This Code substantially complies with
the requirements of the Act,
and
legislative staff,
ident-
ified as "designated employees", are now filing disclosure
statements pursuant to the provisions of the Resolution.
On September 17, 1976, the Attorney General issued
Opinion Nor cv 75/105 (copy attached) concluding that:
l.
The Legislature is an "agency" within
the meaning of Government Code scCtJon
87300 and
must adopt a Conflict of Interest code.
2.

to submit
its Conflict of Interest Code

pursuant to Government code section ducal

Dear Mr. Williams:
In early April of this year, we discussed, with the Speaker
of the Assembly and the President Pro Ten: of
the Senate,
the
applicability of the political Reform Act, which requires adcp~
tlun of a Conflict of Interest Code by all state agencies, to
the California Senate and Assemblyl
At that time,
the proprlety
of the Act requiring submission of the legislative Codes to the
Commission for review and approval was raised.
We agreed tc
seek an Attorney General's opinion on the constitutional issue
raised.
In the meanwhile. Senate Concurrent Resolution
106, which
embodies 3 Conflict of Interest Code for officers and empluyees
of the Legislature,
including the Office of the Auditor Géneral,
was introduced and passed by the Legislature.
This code substan—
tially complies with the requirements of the Act, and legislative
staff,
including staff of the Auditor General,
identified as
"designated employees". are now filing disclosure statements
pursuant to the provisions of this Resolution.
On July 15,
1576,
the Legislative Counsel of California,
in
an opinion requested by Assemblyman Nike Cullen, chairman of the
Joint Legislative Audit committee of the Legislature, stated that
the Office of the Auditor General need not submit
a Conflict of
Interest Code to the Fair Political Practices Commission.
The
Opinion stated that:
The Office of the Auditor General is under the
Control of the Joint Legislative Audit Committee
(Section 1050 31599.), and the Auditor General and
his staff are employees of the committee
tsection
l0504)
.
The office
is
supported from the Contingent

12,

Funds
Of
the Assembly and Senate
in
the
same
manner as other Joint committees of the Leg-
islature
(Section 10504).
Thus,
any discus*
sion of the Legislature's duty to submit
a
Conflict of Interest code is equally appli»
Cable to the Office of the Auditor General.
on September 17, 1975, the Attorney General issued opinion
No. cv 75/105 (copy attached) concluding that:
l.
The Legislature is an "agency" within

and must adopt 3 Conflict of Interest Code.
2i
However,
the Legislature is not required
to submit its Conflict of Interest Code to the
Fair Political Practices Commission for approval
pursuant to Government Code section
37303.

the ConnlSSlon

from its list Of state agencies required

sections.conclusion: The Commission is deleting the California Senate and Assembly from its list of state agencies required to submit Conflict of Interest Codes to the Commission for review and approval, based on the Attorney General's opinion that while the Legislature is an 'agency' that must adopt a Conflict of Interest Code, it is not required to submit such code to the FPPC for approval.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether the California Legislature and legislative agencies (Assembly, Senate, and Office of the Auditor General) must submit their Conflict of Interest Codes to the FPPC for review and approval under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Based on Attorney General Opinion No. CV 75/105, the FPPC concluded that while the Legislature must adopt a Conflict of Interest Code, it is not required to submit that code to the FPPC for approval or review. Therefore, the Commission removed the California Senate, Assembly, and the Office of the Auditor General from its list of agencies required to submit codes for FPPC review.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document consists of three related advice letters with similar structure and conclusion. No formal Q/A section headers, but the substance and purpose are clear. Text quality varies with some OCR errors. Multiple recipients but same core message. Letters reference attached Attorney General Opinion No. CV 75/105 for authority.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the California Legislature and legislative agencies (Assembly, Senate, and Office of the Auditor General) must submit their Conflict of Interest Codes to the FPPC for review and approval under the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: The Commission is deleting the California Senate and Assembly from its list of state agencies required to submit Conflict of Interest Codes to the Commission for review and approval, based on the Attorney General's opinion that while the Legislature is an 'agency' that must adopt a Conflict of Interest Code, it is not required to submit such code to the FPPC for approval.

embedding.summary: This consists of three October 12, 1976 FPPC correspondence letters to the Speaker of the Assembly, the Auditor General, and the Senate Rules Committee Chairman clarifying that the Legislature and its related agencies are not required to submit their Conflict of Interest Codes to the FPPC for approval, following an Attorney General's constitutional opinion on the matter.
